patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 27-year-old woman with sickle cell disease. You are anxious but speak clearly and directly. Start by volunteering: severe pain all over (worst in back/legs) not letting up, started yesterday and worsened overnight; short of breath walking to the bathroom; drinking less due to nausea; you have sickle cell. If asked, describe pain as deep “in my bones,” home meds not helping; low fever 100–101; chest tightness with deep breaths; new cough since this morning; peeing less with darker urine; prior similar admission for pain control; missed a couple hydroxyurea doses; no bowel movement in a few days.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the exact terms "Japanese encephalitis" and "mosquito-borne" and links this to the patient’s exposure risk and neurologic presentation.
Explains treatment as primarily "supportive care" and explicitly includes "seizure control" as a key component.
Performs a critical allergy check before prescribing any medication; if allergy is present, selects an appropriate alternative and documents the rationale.
Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing (non-critical): identifies major interactions (e.g., sedatives/antiepileptics) and adjusts the plan if clinically significant.

Performs a critical contraindication check before ordering/initiating any interventions that carry contraindications (e.g., lumbar puncture in suspected elevated ICP or focal deficits); if contraindicated, defers and escalates care.

Avoids claiming a proven disease-specific antiviral cure for Japanese encephalitis; does not recommend antibiotics/antivirals unless there is a separate indication or uncertainty requiring empiric coverage pending exclusion of treatable causes.
Provides clear return precautions and escalation triggers (worsening consciousness, recurrent seizures, respiratory compromise) and recommends inpatient monitoring when encephalitis is suspected.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose Japanese encephalitis, initiate supportive care with seizure control measures, and urgently refer to neurology/ID/ICU as indicated.
must_collect: recent travel/residence in endemic area or rural exposure
red flags for raised intracranial pressure (severe headache, vomiting, papilledema, focal deficits)
steps: Recognize acute febrile encephalitis syndrome and assess ABCs/need for emergent stabilization.
Explain likely diagnosis: Japanese encephalitis (mosquito-borne) and discuss uncertainty vs other encephalitides.
Perform safety checks: allergy check before prescribing; drug interaction check before prescribing; contraindication check before high-risk procedures (e.g., LP if signs of elevated ICP).
Initiate supportive care plan (airway/oxygenation, fluids/electrolytes, antipyretics, monitoring).
Initiate seizure control plan (start an antiseizure medication per protocol; treat active seizures emergently if present).
Arrange urgent referral/admission with neurology and infectious disease; consider ICU for depressed consciousness, recurrent seizures, or respiratory compromise.

acceptable_variants.description: Uses an alternative first-line antiseizure medication appropriate to context (e.g., lorazepam for active seizure, then fosphenytoin/valproate/levetiracetam) while still emphasizing supportive care and referral.
steps: Record diagnosis: Japanese encephalitis.
Prescribe supportive care measures and an appropriate seizure regimen tailored to whether seizures are active vs prophylaxis.
Refer urgently to neurology/ID and admit for monitoring/ICU as indicated.

acceptable_variants.description: Documents broad differential before finalizing diagnosis (e.g., HSV encephalitis, cerebral malaria, bacterial meningitis) but still records Japanese encephalitis as primary and proceeds with supportive care and referral.
steps: Record differential diagnoses relevant to acute encephalitis and epidemiology.
Record diagnosis: Japanese encephalitis (most likely based on exposure and presentation).
Prescribe supportive care and seizure control plan with required safety checks.
Refer urgently to neurology/ID/ICU.
